question: What is a data structure, and why is the choice of data structure important when designing a program?
expectedPoints: A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data.
The choice affects the efficiency (time and space complexity) of algorithms that operate on the data.
Different structures are suited to different types of applications and operations (e.g., frequent insertion vs. frequent search).

question: Compare and contrast a stack and a queue. Describe the fundamental operations for each and provide a real-world example of their use.
expectedPoints: A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) structure, while a queue is a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) structure.
Stack operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top). Queue operations: enqueue (add to rear), dequeue (remove from front).
Example: A stack is used for undo functionality in an application. A queue is used for printing tasks (a print queue).

question: Explain the process of inserting a new node into a sorted singly linked list. Your answer should include the algorithmic steps and a discussion of the best-case and worst-case time complexity.
expectedPoints: Algorithm: 1) Traverse the list until you find the node where the next node's value is greater than the new value. 2) Set the new node's 'next' pointer to the current node's next node. 3) Set the current node's 'next' pointer to the new node. (Special case: insertion at the head requires updating the head pointer).
Best-case time complexity is O(1) if inserting at the head.
Worst-case time complexity is O(n) if inserting at the end, as it requires traversing the entire list.
